coredata.dc:title: An optical-nose device based on fluorescent nanomaterials sensor array for authentication of saffron
coredata.dc:creator: Masoomi, Sholeh
Sharifi, Hoda
Hemmateenejad, Bahram
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents the development of an optical-nose (o-nose) fluorescence sensor array for the authentication of saffron. The sensor array, which consists of fluorescence sensors made from chemically modified metallic nanoclusters and carbon dots of various functional groups, sniffs the odor emitted from saffron and other possible adulterant herbs and accordingly produces differential fluorescence patterns for different herbs. According to the difference in the chemical composition of the herbs’ aroma, the device was able to differentiate saffron from other herbs and pure saffron from its mixtures with other herbs. The device produces a multi-dimensional response vector and hence it needs to be processed by multivariate data analysis methods. Classification modeling by linear discriminant analysis, principal component-discriminant analysis, and partial least squares discriminant analysis were successfully utilized to model the sensor data and accurate discriminate models were produced. Additionally, the method was able to differentiate between four different organs of saffron which differ in quality. The results of this study demonstrate the potential of o-nose fluorescence sensor arrays for the rapid and reliable authentication of saffron.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical-nose
Fluorescence sensor array
Saffron authentication
Chemometrics
Nanoclusters
Carbon dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design principle of the sensor array
Discriminant analysis of sonochemical extracted saffron from other herbs
Discriminant analysis of untreated solid samples
Discriminant analysis of saffron samples mixed with other herbs
discriminant analysis of different parts of the saffron flower

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thankfully acknowledge financial support from Shiraz University Research Council . We are also thankful to Mr. D. Bazargan (Negin Saffron Collection of Khorasan Company) Bardaskan, Iran for his help in collecting the saffron samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real image of the prepared device and the location of each sensor element on the micro-PAD with the types of indicators. S1: carbon quantum dots, S2: Amine-functionalized carbon dots, S3: Mn (II)-doped carbon dots, S4: Lysozyme-stabilized gold nanoclusters at pH 12, S5: Carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots, S6: Chicken Egg White-stabilized gold nanoclusters, S7: Lysozyme-stabilized gold/silver nanoclusters, S8: H2SO4-carbon dots, S9: Bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters, S10: Glutathione-stabilized gold nanoclusters, S11: Cysteine-stabilized copper nanoclusters, S12: Bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold/silver nanoclusters, S13: Lysozyme-stabilized gold nanoclusters at pH 3.0, S14: nitrogen-doped carbon dots, and S15: Bovine serum albumin-stabilized cadmium nanoclusters.

Effect of different experimental parameters on the accuracy and discrimination ability of the sensor for the extracted and untreated solid samples: (A and AA) effect of temperature for extracted samples, (B and BB) effect of measuring time for extracted samples, (C and CC) effect of the amount of the samples used for extraction, (D and DD) effect of measuring time for solid untreated samples, and (E and EE) effect of the amount of the solid samples.

Right: the images of the sensor array before and after interacting with saffron (A) and other possible adulterant herbs (B), and left: the corresponding colorimetric difference maps. The sensors’ images are shown for just one among five replicated measurements whereas the color difference maps are the average of five replicated measurements.

A graph showing the score plots obtained from (A) PCA, (B) LDA, and (C) PLS-DA, used for clustering and classifying saffron from possible adulterant herbs. (D) A table showing the accuracy obtained from the three methods. (E) A bar graph displaying the device response, represented by the Euclidean norm of the RGB color values of each sensing element, shows the cross-selectivity of the sensing elements for different samples.

The PCA plot of saffron mixed with different concentrations of adulterants in (A) liquid state and (B) solid state. The used adulterants include Safflower, calendula, curcumin, sumac, Rubia, pomegranate flower, and paprika. The numbers denote weight ratio of the adulterants to saffron in the mixtures. Ellipses indicate 95% confidence.
Categories of saffron (A) and category classification by PCA using extracted samples (B) and untreated solid samples (C). Ellipses indicate 95% confidence.

head.ce:title: An optical-nose device based on fluorescent nanomaterials sensor array for authentication of saffron

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71348-14336, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents the development of an optical-nose (o-nose) fluorescence sensor array for the authentication of saffron. The sensor array, which consists of fluorescence sensors made from chemically modified metallic nanoclusters and carbon dots of various functional groups, sniffs the odor emitted from saffron and other possible adulterant herbs and accordingly produces differential fluorescence patterns for different herbs. According to the difference in the chemical composition of the herbs’ aroma, the device was able to differentiate saffron from other herbs and pure saffron from its mixtures with other herbs. The device produces a multi-dimensional response vector and hence it needs to be processed by multivariate data analysis methods. Classification modeling by linear discriminant analysis, principal component-discriminant analysis, and partial least squares discriminant analysis were successfully utilized to model the sensor data and accurate discriminate models were produced. Additionally, the method was able to differentiate between four different organs of saffron which differ in quality. The results of this study demonstrate the potential of o-nose fluorescence sensor arrays for the rapid and reliable authentication of saffron.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Saffron, which is derived from the red-dried stigma of the Crocus sativus L. plant in the Iridaceae family, is among the most expensive plants in the world. It is valued for its color, odor, and various compounds, making it useful in many foods, cosmetics, and pharmaceutical applications [1]. Due to the intensive labor associated with cultivation and harvesting, it is considered a low yield per farmed area [2], and because of this, saffron is known as "red gold" due to its limited availability. It is grown in various countries worldwide, often on small to medium-sized farms and factories, leading to significant variation in saffron quality globally [3]. It takes about 5 kg of wet saffron to produce one to two hundred thousand saffron flowers, and this amount decreases to 1 kg after drying. Saffron is produced in several countries, with Iran being the main producer, accounting for 90% of the world's production of 220 tones [4].

The three primary biologically active compounds that determine saffron's quality are 1) crocins, which are a hydrophilic type of glycoside ester derived from the carotenoid crocetin, as the primary active component in saffron is responsible for its color, as well as its use in the medical and food industries; 2) safranal, which are a glycoside terpenoid, have been reported to exhibit phenomenal pharmacological activities in humans. Odor and aroma of saffron is due to the presence of safranal; and 3) picrocrocin is another important constituent that is responsible for the aroma of saffron [5]. The presence of adulterants in saffron can lower the concentration of these primary biological compounds per unit volume, decreasing quality [6].

Saffron as an expensive spice, is commonly adulterated with lower-quality materials, including the use of artificial colors or other plant materials with similar chemical composition derivatives from flowers of other plants [7]. These plants include sumac powder (SUM), calendula (CAL), rubia powder (RUB), pomegranate flower (PoF), and curcumin (CUR), to increase its weight to increase the mass of the final product without adding costly pure saffron. Also, some adulterants like Safflower (SAF) stamens, dyed lotus stamens, and stigmas of Zea mays L. (corn stigmas) have similar appearance to pure saffron, making it challenging for non-professionals to confirm the authenticity of saffron [8]. The adulterated saffron is then sold at the same price as pure saffron, maximizing profits. These practices diminish the quality, reputation and effectiveness of saffron [9–12].

According to the growing interest in saffron adulteration, there is a high demand for a fast and reliable method for authenticating saffron in the food industry. With its high cost, there is a high risk of adulteration, which poses challenges regarding its traceability and authenticity. ISO 3632 standards categorize saffron into three quality levels based on spectrophotometric analysis data. The findings from this study could help select and implement appropriate quality control techniques to ensure the quality and authenticity of saffron [3,7].

Various analytical techniques have been used for saffron authentication, including spectroscopic techniques such as nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [13], mid infrared (MIR) [14], and ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) [15], as well as chromatographic techniques like gas chromatography (GC) [16], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [17], high performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) [9], ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography/diode-array detector/mass spectrometry (UHPLC-DAD-MS) [18], head-space gas-chromatography-flame ionization detector (HS-GCFID) [7], and thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [19]. These techniques have been used to analyze the main chemical components of saffron for quality control purposes [20]. While these methods exhibit a high level of selectivity that is created by customizing the sensing materials for a specific analyte, they invariably involve laborious, time-consuming, and necessitate trained personnel [21,22]. Therefore, a rapid, precise, and dependable technique is required for saffron quality control, especially with point-of-need capability [23].

In recent years, attention has been directed toward using sensor arrays, that mimic the olfactory or gustatory systems of mammals. Sensor arrays are built by combining several sensors that are not specifically designed but have been extensively utilized in various fields for analyzing a range of materials [24]. The detection of analytes is achieved by aggregating the non-specific responses of all the sensors, which makes designing and constructing sensing systems based on sensor arrays more efficient in terms of time and labor [25,26]. Fluorescent probes are a sensitive and uncomplicated method for detecting specific substances, especially mixtures with multiple substances. Sensor arrays with numerous sensing elements can address this issue by allowing the simultaneous analysis of several objects [27]. The remarkable optical characteristics and numerous surface functional groups of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) [28] and nanoclusters, plus their favorable characteristics such as strong resistance to light-induced degradation, high degree of compatibility with biological systems, and impressive light-emitting properties [29], have made them a popular choice for creating sensor arrays in a straightforward manner [30]. Accordingly, we have made many different fluorescent sensor array sniffing devices based on nanoclusters for the identification of biogenic amines [31,32] as well as the detection of malignancy in gastric cancer tissues [29].

In these miniaturized devices, unique optical response patterns were generated and analyzed using chemometric methods including principal component analysis (PCA) as an unsupervised model and supervised models such as linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA).

In this assay, we have reported an optical-nose sniffing device by an array of fluorescent metallic nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots deposited on a paper substrate. This device was successfully employed for the quality control monitoring of saffron. The device worked based on reacting with the volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emitted from the samples in their headspace. The different nanomaterials used interact differentially with the chemical constituents of the samples and therefore provide a unique colorimetric pattern for each type of sample. The results showed that combining the paper-based optical sensor array with chemometric methods can provide a rapid, non-destructive, and low-cost screening method for the authentication of saffron. The objective of the research is to develop a method for identifying and quantifying the adulteration of saffron caused by these biological and chemical adulterants. Accordingly, we investigated the adulteration of saffron with seven specific bio-adulterants that originate from plants, which were Safflower, calendula, curcumin, sumac, rubia, pomegranate flower, and paprika.
The information regarding “Reagents”, “Apparatus and Software”, and “Preparation of saffron aqueous extract” is provided in the supplementary section.

An optical-nose PAD (paper-based analytical device) was fabricated by depositing the solutions of 15 fluorescent NCs and CDs ( Fig. 1). Several factors were considered when selecting the appropriate design for the PAD. These factors included the need for an even number of zones (which ruled out a triangular design) and the size and shape of the container cap (which ruled out rectangular and elongated designs). Additionally, ease of cutting (which ruled out circular and six-corner designs) and the need to minimize mistakes during injection since sensor solutions were colorless (which ruled out completely similar designs, such as a square). Ultimately, the 5-sided face PAD design was chosen as the most suitable option (Fig. 1). Every sensing zone was indicated by a solid circle.

Using AutoCAD, a five-sided patterned paper with sixteen zones was created (One zone was selected for marking, and no sensor was injected into it). The design was then printed on sheets of Whatman grade 2 filter paper. To each circle zone, except that on the top, a 0.3 µL portion of an individual fluorescent nanomaterial as a sensing element was added and let to dry. The positions of the added nanomaterials are indicated in Fig. 1. The real fluorescence image of the device after fabrication is also given in this Figure.

A homemade fluorescent imaging cabinet equipped with two UV fluorescent lamps of 254 and 366 nm was used to capture the fluorescence image of the PADs. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence image of every PAD was captured using a smartphone camera before interacting with the VOCs in the headspace of the samples (before or reference image). For measuring the extracted samples in the liquid phase, a 500 µL portion of the extracted sample solution was placed in a glass vessel, and the sensor array-mounted cap was closed. The VOCs released from the sample were exposed to the PAD for 1.5 h at 60 °C. In the same way, as was described for the reference image, the fluorescence image of the PAD was again captured (after the image).

For measuring the untreated samples in solid form, a 20 mg portion of the sample was placed in a container. The cap containing the sensor array PAD was then sealed shut. The PAD was exposed to the released VOCs of the sample for 9 h at room temperature.

Using ImageJ, the average RGB color intensities in the RGB color model were calculated over all pixels for each fluorescence spot (sensing area) on the sensor array. The average of changes in color intensities between the device’s images taken before and after exposure to the sample's vapor was then computed. The digital color difference data were gathered for every sample in a data vector of length 45 (15 sensors × 3 RGB values). The digital color difference data ranged from − 255 to 255, where negative values mean decreasing color intensity after exposure to the samples’ VOCs and vice versa. The response of the devices to all samples was collected in data matrices sizing (n × 45), where n is the number of studied samples. For extracting the patterns and clusters from the data matrices, different pattern recognition approaches, including PCA, LDA, and PLS-DA, were employed.

PCA, as a mathematical projection technique, transforms the original data matrix into a matrix of lower dimension with orthogonal vectors. PCA transformation comes with some benefits, namely the possibility of data visualization in lower dimensions, noise reduction, and removing correlation among the variables by producing orthogonal vectors, which are more suitable for future modeling like regression and classification. [33]. To mitigate the effects of the various dimensions, the data was first standardized, and the most effective principal component (PC) values were chosen to be input into the discriminating analysis. One of the most popular classification techniques is LDA, which maximizes the between-group variance while simultaneously minimizing the within-group variance. In this method, the group to which the samples belong is determined [34]. The PLS-DA, another approach to PCA-LDA, is one of the most commonly used supervised learning methods for modeling data matrices' colinear variables. In this method, the extracted factors are rotated such that, while preserving the higher variances of the original data, they also produce the best separation across classes. An important issue in PLS-DA is selecting the most appropriate number of PLS latent variables to avoid overfitting. Here, the model refinement procedure used cross-validation to select optimum number of latent variables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: The NCs and CDs of the sensor array can interact with the chemical compounds emitted from the saffron and adulterant samples. This can change the fluorescence properties of NCs and CDs (quenching or enhancing fluorescence intensity or changing excitation or emission peaks). Numerous investigations have shown that when exposed to various organic solvents, metal NCs and CDs exhibit clear solvent-dependent photoluminescence capabilities, which are attributed to the rich surface features of these sensing materials [35,36]. The optical characteristics of the metal NCs and CDs could be altered if their chemical environment changes because it could lead to electronic energy splitting and electron redistribution on the surface of NCs and CDs [37]. Due to the difference in nature or concentration of VOCs released by both saffron and adulterated samples, changes in the fluorescence intensity of the sensors, leading to discrimination between saffron and adulterated samples, are expected.

The fluorescence of NCs is affected more by compounds that contain ketone and aldehyde functional groups and nitrogen heteroatoms [38]. The protein and amino acid shielding covering the surface of the NCs plays a crucial role in their fluorescence, and this shielding can be altered by reactive functional groups that react with the surrounding amino groups. Saffron contains many compounds from various chemical classes, including aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, and hydrocarbons [39–41]. Safranal (2,6,6 trimethyl-1,4-cyclohexadiene-1-carboxaldehyde) is the primary volatile compound in saffron [42], which has an aldehyde group in its structure. The results of our previous research [29] showed that the organic functional groups (such as -COOH, -NH2, -CO, -SH, and -CO-NH-, N-H, CC, and -OH) present in CDs and NCs could have non-covalent interactions with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing VOCs, leading to the aggregation of these particles and altering their fluorescence properties [43–45].

Noble metal NCs, such as those made from Au, Cu, and Ag, as well as Cd, are brand-new classes of luminous nanomaterials with distinctly molecule-like properties and discrete electronic energy levels. These metal NCs have strong photobleaching resistance, a large Stokes shift, great dispersion, and no blinking [35,46]. Additionally, both the metal core and the capping agent can be changed to alter the interaction of VOCs with NCs [47].

Therefore, we aimed to provide a whole platform for the various noncovalent interactions of the released VOCs from saffron and other related herbs with sensors. To achieve this, we used two proteins and three amino acids as capping agents, and four different metallic cores as NC-based sensing platforms. Additionally, we synthesized some CDs with different functional groups using varying precursors and synthesis methods, resulting in several organic molecules fluorescent quantum dots.

As observed in Fig. 1, our optical E-nose system consisted of nine nanoclusters including lysozyme-capped Au NCs at pH 12.0 (Lys-Au-12 NCs), lysozyme-capped Au NCs at pH 3.0 (Lys-Au-3 NCs), Au NCs modified with chicken egg white protein (CEW-Au NCs), Au NCs modified with bovine serum albumin (BSA-Au NCs), Au NCs modified with glutathione (GSH-Au NCs), Cu NCs modified with cysteine (Cys-Cu NCs), Cd NCs modified with BSA (BSA-Cd NCs), bimetallic Au/Ag NCs modified with lysozyme (Lys-Ag/Au NCs), and bimetallic Au/Ag NCs modified with BSA (BSA-Ag/Au NCs). The detailed synthetic procedure for these nanoclusters has been described in previous work conducted by our research group [29]. Nevertheless, the synthesis method for these NCs is provided in the supplementary section.

Furthermore, our o-nose system consisted of six fluorescence carbon dots (CDs), such as NH2-CD, Mn-CD, H2SO4-CD, N-CD, COOH-CD, and CQD. Carbon dots exhibit environmentally friendly properties, as they do not contain heavy metal elements [48]. Carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (COOH-CD) were produced through a microwave-assisted technique using citric acid as the starting material [49]. The core is terminated at the edge by heteroatomic functional groups that constitute the surface area. In pure CDs, these groups are mostly −OH, −CO, and −COOH, whereas in N- or S-doped CDs, amine and thiol-like groups are also present. This surface region of the CD comprises functionalized units behaving like small organic molecule fluorophores [50]. To create amine-functionalized carbon dots (NH2-CD), EDA was introduced into a solution containing pre-existing COOH-CD [51]. Nanocarbon dots (N-CDs) display adjustable emission properties in solvents with different polarities attributed to hydrogen bonding between VOCs and N-CDs. The N-CDs were synthesized with o-Phenylenediamine via a facile hydrothermal method [48]. Doping metals in carbon dots can alter their photoluminescence properties. Therefore, the addition of manganese chloride to carbon dots resulted in the development of a metal-related energy-acceptor fluorophore in Mn (II)-doped carbon dots (Mn-CD) [52]. H2SO4-CD was prepared through a one-step hydrothermal heating process of o-phenylenediamine in a H2SO4 solution [53]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using citric acid and urea as precursor materials. The resultant CQDs emit a bright green fluorescence when irradiated with UV light and contain functional groups such as CO, –NH, C–N, and C–O [54].

Fig. S2 shows the optical absorption and emission spectra of the used CDs at different excitation wavelengths. All the CDs exhibited intense absorption in the ultraviolet region and exhibited intrinsic fluorescence emission when excited at around 300 nm. For the characterization of CDs, the morphology and structure of the three CDs were analyzed using TEM (Fig. S2), showing dot particles in the size of 10 nm or smaller. As shown in Fig. S3, FT-IR spectra were recorded to determine the functional groups on the surface of CDs and NCs. All nanomaterials exhibited a broad peak at ∼3400 cm-1 corresponding to the O-H or N-H stretching vibration attributed to carboxylic acid and amine groups, and displayed C-H vibration at ∼2920 cm-1. Also, the spectra are rich in vibration bands in the range of 1000–2000 cm-1, explaining the presence of carboxylic, carbonyl, and amide groups on the surface of the studied nanomaterials. An interesting observation is significant differences in the peak intensities in this wavelength region, which can be attributed to difference in the surface functional groups of the nanomaterials, result in differential interactions with the VOCs of saffron and other studied herbs.

This research aimed to use a specially designed o-nose device to analyze 15 saffron samples from various regions of Iran, as well as 8 plant materials and 2 synthetic dyes that were used as possible saffron adulterants. The study involved analyzing these samples to distinguish between authenticated saffron and adulterated samples in liquid and solid forms. The responses of the sensor to saffron and four possible adulterants: CUR, CAL, PAP, and SAF (with five replicates for each), were recorded at different experimental conditions. Then the data were subjected to PCA-LDA analysis, and model accuracy to discriminate the authenticated saffron from the adulterants was used as the objective function. In addition, discrimination ability (DA), computed as the ratio of the variance between groups to the variance within groups (Eq. S1) [55], was used to assess the effect of experimental factors.

The performance of the sensor array is influenced by several experimental factors, including temperature, concentrations of sample extracts for liquid phase, amount of solid samples, and measurement time. The supplementary material section provides detailed information on the optimization process. PCA plots of discrimination samples at different temperatures and times are represented in Figs. S4 and S5, respectively. Also, PCA plot of samples in different concentration is shown in Fig. S6. Finally, PCA plots of the discrimination of untreated solid samples at various times and concentrations are shown in Figs. S7 and S8, respectively. The optimized condition involved analyzing the sample extract at a concentration of 50 mg mL-1, at a temperature of 60 ˚C, with a measurement time of 1.5 h. Additionally, for untreated solid samples, it was observed that using 20 mg of samples provided optimal discrimination of saffron from other adulterants after 9 h. Bar plots displaying the accuracy and discrimination ability of different experimental factors for the extracted and untreated solid samples are shown in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminant analysis of sonochemical extracted saffron from other herbs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the ability of the device to identify saffron among all the different adulterant herbs was investigated. The images of the device before and after interacting with the extracted solutions of the herbs are given in Fig. 3. The color difference maps (CDMs), calculated by taking the difference in the color intensities of the after and before images, clearly demonstrate the unique fingerprint of the device for saffron compared to other herbs. It should be noted that each sample was tested in five parallel measurements, and the CDMs shown in Fig. 3 are the average of the repeated measurements.

According to the CMDs shown in Fig. 3, the sensors S9 (BSA-Au NCs), S12 (BSA-Au/Ag NCS), S14 (N-CDs), and S15 (BSA-Cd NCs) represent the visual difference in the color change of the sensor array to saffron comparing to the other herbs. According to these observations, the BSA-modified NCs represented a greater impact on discrimination. In other words, the VOCs emitted from the saffron and non-saffron herbs represented a differential interaction with BSA compared to other chemicals used to modify the surfaces of the NCs. BSA, as a complex macromolecule, can have many different physicochemical interactions with the small molecules, ranging from Coulombic interaction, hydrogen bonding, and polar and hydrophobic interactions. Accordingly, it can add extra selectivity to the sensing system.

The observed cross-selectivity of the studied nanomaterials can be explained using their surface functional groups unraveled by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S3). CDs have two-domain structures, with a carbon-rich core and a hydrophilic shell. Nitrogen and oxygen-containing shells, like NH2-CD and N-CD, can enhance the electron-donating capability of CDs due to their highly electronegative nature and electron pair [56]. These shells are crucial for adsorption and improve electrostatic interactions with VOCs [57]. Their electronic behavior can be changed based on the polarity of the environment and VOC-shell chemical interactions, including different strengths of H-bonding [58]. As one can see from CDMs, the NH2-CD showed strong and similar responses against all analytes. This may weaken the array's ability to distinguish saffron from its adulterations. However, in N-CD, nitrogen is present in the form of secondary and tertiary amines. Although the steric effect of these functional groups could make the reaction conditions somewhat difficult against some of the existing VOCs, it was beneficial for the discrimination between analytes and improvement of the array’s performance.

In addition, a dilute solution of saffron (0.013 mg mL-1) was prepared and exposed to UV light to check its fluorescence emission. Next, 20 µL of saffron solution was added to 2 mL of each nano-sensors’ solutions to record the effect of saffron on their fluorescence emission. As shown in Fig. S9 , the saffron solution, which represented a very weak emission peak at around 600 nm, reduced the intensity of the fluorescence emission of almost all sensors, except GSH-Au and MnCD. In some cases, the fluorescent peak of the nanomaterials was shifted to the left, such as the NH2-CD and Cys-Cu sensors or to the right (BSA-Cd) after addition of saffron. This differential interaction of saffron chemical constituents with the nano-sensors can be considered as the basis of sensor array cross-selectivity.

Interaction of the nanomaterials with VOCs of saffron was also investigated by recording solid state ATR-FTIR spectra of the deposited nanomaterials on the paper surface before and after interacting with the VOCs (Fig. S10). Two new vibrational bands are observed at around 1000 cm-1 and 500 cm-1, which can be attributed to the C-H aromatic and C-O-C vibrations of the saffron chemical constitutes.

The color difference data were then analyzed by supervised and unsupervised learning methods to derive mathematical models for classification. Considering all types of samples, there are a total of 15 saffron and 9 adulterated samples. Considering five repeated measurements, a total of 120 measurements were done. Therefore, a data matrix sizing (120 ×45) was obtained, which was analyzed using PCA, LDA, and PLS-DA. The classification models were run for the two-class problem, e.g., discrimination of authenticated saffron samples from the other studied herbs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the saffron and non-saffron herbs were clustered obviously in the factor space of the first 3 PCA scores, explaining cumulatively 88% of the variances in the original data. Feeding the PCA scores into the LDA model resulted in a discriminant model with high classification accuracy.

Recent studies indicate the interest of researchers in using fluorescence assay based on nanomaterials to confirm the authenticity of saffron. For example, Long et al. [12] developed a 3D fluorescence sensing system using a hydrophilic hydrazine-naphthalimide functionalized chitosan (HN-chitosan) polymer probe, which combined with chemometrics to detect potential adulteration in saffron. Based on the obtained results, Crocin-I as the main active non-volatile ingredient in saffron could react the probe's amino functional group, amplifying the fluorescence signal difference between adulterants and saffron. Also, in another study [59], bright green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method and used for the sensitive detection of crocin. Crocin has been found to significantly decrease the fluorescence of SiNPs. Compared to the studies mentioned above, very small volume of reagents and analytes are used in our method. In this method, it is also possible to analyze the saffron sample in its solid state without any manipulation. In addition, fluorescence changes have been recorded by a mobile phone as a cheap, portable, and ubiquitous device, eliminating the need for expensive fluorescence instruments. In this proposed method, an array of fluorescent nanomaterials is used and the amount of error resulting from the presence of interferences is minimized.

As shown in Fig. 4 B, the LDA score represented a significant difference between the saffron and non-saffron herbs. It should be noted that the number of LDA scores is one unit less than the number of classes and for a two-class problem, there is just one LDA score for every sample. Assessing the LDA model by cross-validation resulted in 98% classification accuracy.

The data was also analyzed using PLS-DA (see Fig. 4 C), which is capable of modeling collinear data and handling small nonlinearities. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the data were scattered in two distinct classes in the factor space of the PLS latent variables and as shown in part D of Fig. 4, PLS-DA reported a 100% classification accuracy for both training and cross-validation.

To explore the selectivity of the sensing elements of the sensor array for discrimination, the overall response of the sensing device for all samples in each group (saffron and non-saffron) was calculated as the Euclidean norm and represented as a bar plot (Fig. 4 E). As shown in Fig. 4 E, the bar plot provided a concise overview of the cross-selectivity of the sensing elements used to authenticate saffron. Sensors 2, 5, 9, 12, and 15 displayed a greater response to saffron compared to adulterated samples. Conversely, adulterated samples exhibited a stronger inclination to react with sensors 3, 8, 10, and 14, resulting in saffron discrimination. The statistical parameters for the validated discriminant models of saffron and adulterants can be found in Table S2, reporting 100%, 96% and 97% cross-validation sensitivity, specificity and precision for saffron identification, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminant analysis of untreated solid samples
ce:section.ce:para: The next step involved evaluating the ability of our sensing device to identify saffron from the non-saffron herbs based on the untreated solids from the herbs. To achieve this, each sample was tested at least five times, and the results from these repeated measurements were used to create a data matrix of size (120 ×45), which was then analyzed using PCA, LDA, and PLS-DA. The accuracy obtained through these methods is presented in Fig. S10 (D), where the LDA method was able to distinguish between saffron and adulterated samples with an 83% accuracy rate. Additionally, the data was analyzed using a PCA, showing three distinct clusters in the factor space (see Fig. S10 (C)). The average CDMs for all samples in each group are displayed in Fig. S10 (F).

In general, the results obtained in this section showed that the proposed o-nose, in addition to the liquid phase, was able to separate saffron in the solid phase (without destroying the sample) with acceptable accuracy from adulterant materials. However, for more accurate analysis, using extracted samples is recommended. The bar plot in Fig. S10 (E) provides a clear overview of the cross-selectivity of the sensing elements used to authenticate saffron. It can be seen from this Figure that almost all the sensing elements showed a greater response to adulterant than saffron.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminant analysis of saffron samples mixed with other herbs
ce:section.ce:para: Besides successful discrimination of pure saffron from the pure non-saffron herbs, the o-nose device was employed to identify pure saffron from the saffron mixed with the other possible adulterants. From a practical point of view, this investigation is of high importance since usually adulterated saffron samples are prepared by mixing the saffron with herbs, where the smell and color of the saffron are obvious in the product.

A mixture of all the studied non-saffron herbs was prepared by mixing equal amounts of these herbs. Then, this mixture of the herbs was mixed with pure saffron at different weight compositions: 5:100 (0.05 Wm/Ws), 10:100 (0.10 Wm/Ws), 25:100 (0.25 Wm/Ws), 50:100 (0.50 Wm/Ws), 75:100 (0.75 Wm/Ws), and 100:100 (1.0 Wm/Ws), where "m" and "s" subscripts refer to the mixture of adulterants and saffron, respectively. The final mixture was then tested with the sensor array, and the resultant data were analyzed using PCA for both sonochemically extracted samples and untreated solid samples. Scatter plots of the data in the factor space of PCA scores are given in Fig. 5. As evident from both plots, the samples with different composition percentages are well separated in solid and liquid states, indicating the high capability of the designed o-nose sensor array in authenticating saffron. Modeling of the PCA scores by the LDA method resulted in discriminant models, which were able to classify the studied groups with 100% accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: discriminant analysis of different parts of the saffron flower
ce:section.ce:para: The saffron flower is divided into four main parts: Sargol, Pushal, Bunch, and Risheh (Root). The quality of saffron depends on the harvested part, where Sargol corresponds to the finest category according to ISO 3632. Sargol saffron consists of only the dark red stigma, while Pushal saffron is the stigma part of the plant attached to a 1–3 mm style end. Bunch saffron includes red stigma and a significant amount of yellow style, whereas Root saffron is the yellow part of the plant. These saffron categories are depicted in Fig. 6 A. Discrimination between Sargol and Pushal is extremely difficult, and only highly skilled experts can differentiate them. A Bunch may also be sold instead of Pushal. So, having simple-to-use devices that can discriminate between these parts of saffron is of special importance, especially from an economic point of view.

In this part, the o-nose device was employed to differentiate these different parts of the saffron. For each part, two different authenticated samples were prepared and measured by the sensor array device via five replicate runs. As demonstrated by the PCA plots in Fig. 6 B and C (that represented saffron extract and untreated solid samples, respectively), the device represented promising results for the discrimination of the saffron body members. Interestingly, Sargol, which possesses the highest quality, is separated far from the organs, explaining the potential of the sensing device to identify this valuable organ from other lower quality organs.

This research developed an optical-nose device based on fluorescent metallic nanoclusters and carbon quantum dots for quality monitoring of saffron. By combining the sensing device with chemometrics tools, we could differentiate and distinguish the high-quality saffron from the adulterated samples. The sensing device, which worked based on sniffing the odors emitted from the studied herbs, represented differential fluorescence patterns for the saffron and other herbs. The developed method was effective in discriminating the pure saffron samples with its mixtures with other herbs in both untreated solid state and sonochemically water-extracted liquid state. Additionally, the method was able to differentiate between different saffron organs. Our untargeted metabolomics approach reported the same accuracy as the method measured single metabolite, e.g., those measured crocin as potential biomarker [12,59]. However, our method can detect imbalances in metabolomics profile of the saffron samples (not just a single species) and hence are more sensitive to adulteration. Furthermore, the reported device here is portable and can be used by non-expert users for point-of-need testing.
Hemmateenejad Bahram: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Sharifi Hoda: Validation, Conceptualization. Masoomi Sholeh: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thankfully acknowledge financial support from Shiraz University Research Council. We are also thankful to Mr. D. Bazargan (Negin Saffron Collection of Khorasan Company) Bardaskan, Iran for his help in collecting the saffron samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135365.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, X. Teng, X. Sun, W. Pan, J. Wang, Carbon dots with aggregation induced quenching effect and solvatochromism for the detection of H2O in organic solvents, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 267(2022) 120547.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical detection of anthrax biomarkers in an aqueous medium: The combination of carbon quantum dots and europium ions within alginate hydrogels
J. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, Z. Wang, Y. Tang, Y. Zheng, Q. Wang, Optical detection of anthrax biomarkers in an aqueous medium: The combination of carbon quantum dots and europium ions within alginate hydrogels, Journal of Materials Science, 54(2019) 2526–2534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An array of metallic nanozymes can discriminate and detect a large number of anions
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sharifi, J. Tashkhourian, B. Hemmateenejad, An array of metallic nanozymes can discriminate and detect a large number of anions, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 339(2021) 129911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water decontamination using bio-based, chemically functionalized, doped, and ionic liquid-enhanced adsorbents
Environ. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.N. Zare, A. Mudhoo, M.A. Khan, M. Otero, Z.M.A. Bundhoo, C. Navarathna, et al., Water decontamination using bio-based, chemically functionalized, doped, and ionic liquid-enhanced adsorbents, Environmental Chemistry Letters, 19(2021) 3075–3114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adsorption of VOCs onto engineered carbon materials: A review
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, B. Gao, A.E. Creamer, C. Cao, Y. Li, Adsorption of VOCs onto engineered carbon materials: A review, Journal of hazardous materials, 338(2017) 102–123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots detect water-to-ice phase transition and act as alcohol sensors via fluorescence turn-off/on mechanism
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kalytchuk, L. Zdrazil, Zk Bad’ura, M. Medved’, M. Langer, M. Paloncyova, et al., Carbon dots detect water-to-ice phase transition and act as alcohol sensors via fluorescence turn-off/on mechanism, ACS Nano, 15(2021) 6582–6593.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green-emitting silicon nanoparticles as a fluorescent probe for highly-sensitive crocin detection and pH sensing
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Pan, Q. Wen, L. Ma, X. Qin, S. Feng, Green-emitting silicon nanoparticles as a fluorescent probe for highly-sensitive crocin detection and pH sensing, New Journal of Chemistry, 46(2022) 12729–12738.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sholeh Masoomi received her B.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from the University of Sistan and Baluchestan, Zahedan, Iran, in 2012, and her M.S. degree in Nano Science and Technology—NanoChemistry from the Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran, in 2014. She started her Ph.D. course at the Department of Chemistry, Shiraz University, in 2016. Her research interests focus on two fields. First, she dedicated her research to the synthesis of a bio-compatible, safe, and inexpensive novel nanocomposite to replace existing hygroscopic and glaciogenic materials and its use in cloud seeding. Second, her research focused on the development of paper-based sensor arrays that utilize indicators, nanoclusters, and carbon dots to detect and discriminate complex food items like honey and saffron using colorimetric and fluorometric methods.

Hoda Sharifi received her B.S. and M.S. degrees in pure chemistry and analytical chemistry from Shahid Chamran University in 2012 and 2015, respectively, and Ph.D. from Shiraz University in 2022. Her research interests focus on the development of paper-based analytical device for monitoring environmental hazardous materials and medical diagnosis. She has spent a ten-months period as a visiting researcher at Heidelberg University on paper-based analytical sensors.

Bahram Hemmateenejad is a professor of Chemistry at Chemistry Department of Shiraz University. He received his B.S. degree in pure chemistry from Shiraz University in 1996, M.S. and PhD in Analytical Chemistry from Isfahan University of Technology and Shiraz University in 1998 and 2002, respectively. As fellow of Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, he has visited different research institutes and universities in Germany. He won many national and international awards such as Razi Festival award, Analytical Chemistry Award of the Iranian Chemical Society, Elsevier Chemolab Award and TWAS young affiliate membership. He runs a Lab on the development and applications of different chemometrics methods in the chemical disciplines of bioanalytical chemistry, chemo-biological interactions, computer-aided drug design and colorimetric sensors. Currently he is very active on development of paper-based analytical sensors. He is associate editor of Journal of the Iran Chemical Society and author of more than 260 research papers and reviews. With an h-index of 44, his name has been appeared among top 2% scientists for 3 consecutive years.
